event: Saint Paul Caught Up to the Third Heaven
symbols: upward-reaching arms — Paul's ecstatic transport into the heavenly realm
angels and cherubim surrounding — the heavenly court receiving Paul
red mantle billowing — the dramatic movement of spiritual rapture
golden light from above — the divine presence drawing Paul upward
clouds — the threshold between earthly and heavenly realms
description: A large, dramatic painting in a gilded frame showing a bearded man in green and red robes with arms raised heavenward in ecstatic rapture. He gazes upward toward an opening of golden light in clouds. Angels, cherubim, and putti surround him — some playing instruments, others reaching toward him. His red mantle billows dramatically. The lower portion shows figures pulling at his garments. The composition swirls upward with intense energy and warm golden light.
theological_description: Paul recounts in 2 Corinthians a mystical experience: 'I know a man in Christ who fourteen years ago was caught up to the third heaven — whether in the body or out of the body I do not know, God knows. And I know that this man was caught up into paradise and heard things that cannot be told, which man may not utter' (2CO.12.2-4). Paul's reticence about this experience ('a man in Christ,' speaking in the third person) paradoxically attests to its profundity — what he witnessed was beyond human language. The third heaven in Jewish cosmology was the highest heaven, the dwelling place of God. This ecstatic vision gave Paul unparalleled authority as an apostle, yet he immediately counters it with his thorn in the flesh (2CO.12.7), demonstrating that divine strength is made perfect in weakness (2CO.12.9). The dramatic composition captures the violence of the transport — this is not serene contemplation but overwhelming divine power seizing a human being.

scripture_refs.reason: Paul's account of being caught up to the third heaven and hearing inexpressible things
The thorn in the flesh and God's grace sufficient in weakness, balancing the ecstatic experience
Paul's earlier encounter with the risen Christ on the Damascus road — a pattern of overwhelming divine revelation
John's similar experience of being caught up to heaven and seeing the throne of God
